back: The probability distribution of a given statistic (e.g., mean, proportion) based on a random sample. It describes the variability of the statistic over all possible samples of the same size from the population.

front: What is the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) and why is it crucial for statistical inference?
back: The CLT states that the sampling distribution of the sample mean will approach a normal distribution as the sample size increases, regardless of the shape of the population distribution. This allows us to use normal probability models to make inferences about population parameters.

front: What is the standard error of the mean (SEM)?
back: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean. It measures the precision of the sample mean as an estimate of the population mean and is calculated as SEM = σ/√n, where σ is the population standard deviation and n is the sample size.

front: How does sample size (n) affect the sampling distribution?
back: As sample size increases: 1) The sampling distribution becomes more normally distributed (per CLT), 2) The spread (standard error) of the distribution decreases, meaning estimates become more precise and less variable.

front: What is the difference between a population parameter and a sample statistic?
back: A parameter is a fixed numerical value describing a characteristic of a population (e.g., population mean μ). A statistic is a value computed from a sample used to estimate a parameter (e.g., sample mean x̄). The sampling distribution describes the behavior of the statistic.
